Christ is God’s beloved Son and our beloved Savior. The Lord's care for the church of Israel is described by how He managed a vineyard. The advantages of our situation will be discussed another day. He planted it with the best vines, gave them an excellent law, and instituted proper ceremonies. The temple was like a tower where God showed His presence. He set up his altar, to which sacrifices should be brought; all the means of grace are represented by this. God expects fruit from those who enjoy privileges. Good intentions and good beginnings are helpful, but not enough; there must be vineyard fruit—thoughts and affections, words and actions that agree with the Spirit. It produced bad fruit. Wild grapes are the fruits of a corrupt nature. Where grace does not work, corruption will. But the wickedness of those who profess religion and enjoy the means of grace is their own fault. They will no longer be a special people. When errors and vice go unchecked, the vineyard is unpruned; then it will soon be overgrown with thorns. This is often shown in God’s Spirit departing from those who have long resisted Him, and His gospel being removed from places that have been a disgrace to it. The explanation is given. It is sad for a soul when, instead of humility, gentleness, love, patience, and disregard for the world, which God desires, there are pride, passion, discontent, malice, and contempt for God; instead of praying and praising, cursing and swearing. Let us produce fruit with patience so that in the end we may obtain everlasting life.

Here is a warning to those who set their hearts on the wealth of the world. It is not wrong for those who have a house and field to purchase another; but the problem is they never know when they have enough. Covetousness is idolatry, and while many envy the prosperous, a wretched man, the Lord declares terrible warnings against him. How applicable this is to many among us! God has many ways to empty even the most populated cities. Those who set their hearts on the world will be disappointed. Here is a warning to those who indulge in pleasures and delights. Enjoying music is lawful; but when it draws your heart away from God, then it becomes a sin. God’s judgments have seized them, but they will not interrupt themselves in their pleasures. The warnings are declared. No matter how high a person may be, death will bring him low; no matter how lowly, death will bring him lower. The result of these judgments will be that God will be glorified as a powerful and holy God; He will be acknowledged and declared to be so through the righteous punishment of proud people. Those who set up sin and try to satisfy their base desires are in a terrible condition. They boldly commit sin, following their own lusts; they mockingly call God the Holy One of Israel. They confuse and destroy the difference between good and evil. They prefer their own reasoning to divine revelation, their own plans to God’s counsel and commands. They think it wise to continue profitable sins and neglect self-denying duties. Also, even though people may not take drunkenness seriously, it is a sin that exposes them to God's wrath and curse. Their judges perverted justice. Every sin needs another to hide it.

Let no one expect an easy life if they live wickedly. Sin weakens the strength of a people; it ruins the beauty of a people. When God’s word is despised, and His law rejected, what can people expect but that God should completely abandon them? When God comes in wrath, the hills tremble, and fear seizes even great men. When God plans to destroy a provoking people, He can find instruments to do it, just as He sent for the Chaldeans, and later the Romans, to destroy the Jews. Those who would not listen to the voice of God speaking through His prophets will hear the voices of their enemies roaring against them. Let those in distress look wherever they want; everything appears bleak. If God frowns upon us, how can any creature smile? Let us diligently seek a firm assurance that when all earthly help and comfort fails, God Himself will be the strength of our hearts and our portion forever.
